Home·Journal·The kitchen

Sea to table

Nadia Petersen · Head Chef2 June 20266 min read

The catch lands at six in the morning. By the time you sit down to dinner, it's travelled less than a kilometre. That's the whole idea — and the only rule I really care about.

I get to the kitchen before the sun is properly up, while the dining room is still chairs-on-tables and the only sound is the sea through the open doors. The first delivery is already at the back: line fish from the day boats, still cold and bright-eyed, packed in ice that morning. Whatever they caught is what we cook. We write the menu after we see the fish, never before.

Cooking with what the sea gives

It sounds romantic; mostly it's discipline. You can't fall in love with a dish if the fish for it didn't come in. So we keep the cooking simple and let the ingredient lead — yellowtail over coals with nothing but lemon and good oil, mussels steamed in white wine and garden herbs, a whole fish for the table to pull apart with their hands. The kitchen garden out back gives us the rest: tomatoes, herbs, the lemons that go on everything.

A cellar that never leaves the Cape

Everything we pour grows within two hours of the bar. We're spoiled for it — Constantia chardonnay, Stellenbosch reds, the crisp coastal whites from down the coast that taste of exactly this. Our sommelier has one rule, the same as mine: the wine should taste of somewhere, the way the food does.

"You don't need to do much to a fish that good. The skill is knowing when to stop."— Nadia Petersen

By six in the evening the kitchen is loud and the dining room is filling and the light is going gold across the water. This is the part I love — the dishes leaving the pass, the tables by the window watching the sun fall into the sea, everyone slowing down at the same time.

  • The menu changes daily, written around the morning's catch.
  • Almost everything is grown, caught or made within an hour of the door.
  • Tell us your table's a celebration and we'll quietly make it one.

People ask for the recipe sometimes. The honest answer is that there isn't really one. Buy the freshest fish you can find, cook it less than you think, pour something local, and eat it where you can hear the sea. That's the whole secret, and you're welcome to it.

NP
Nadia Petersen
Head Chef · Oceans View
